I used to shop only in Brazil, but now I buy things abroad.
I didn't use to feel comfortable returning things in English.
There used to be a duty-free counter here, but they closed it.
Did you use to keep every receipt?
I used to pay full price. Now I wait for a markdown.
I used to keep things that didn't fit. Now I exchange them.
I didn't use to check the return policy. Now I read it first.
There's a fifteen percent restocking fee.
It was fifty percent off on clearance.
That comes to a hundred and forty dollars.
You have thirty days to return it.
Hi there, welcome to returns. What can I help you with today?
Hi. I'd like to return this jacket. The zipper is broken, so it's defective -- and here's the receipt.
Oh, I'm sorry about that. Let me take a look. Yes, that zipper's completely stuck. Since it's defective, our return policy gives you a full refund, no time limit.
That's good to hear. Actually, I really liked the jacket -- could I exchange it for the same one that isn't broken, instead of a refund?
Of course. Let me check the stock. We do have your size. And because it was defective, there's no restocking fee -- the exchange is completely free.
Thank you. Honestly, I used to just keep things when they broke, because I didn't know how to return them in English. I'm learning.
Well, you're doing great. One more thing -- I see you're in our loyalty program, so I'll keep your points on the exchange. Anything else?
Actually, yes. I also bought this bag, but I found the exact same one cheaper on another website. Do you do a price match?
We do, if it's the identical item. Show me the page? ... Yes, that's the same bag. I'll match it and put the difference back on your card.
That's wonderful -- thank you. Let me just check I got it all: the jacket is exchanged, no fee, and the price on the bag is matched. Is that right?
I'd like to return this -- it's defective. Here's the receipt.
Could I exchange this for a different size?
Do you do a price match? I found it cheaper online.
I understand it's final sale, but it's defective.
Is this your best price?

Hello, this is a message for Lucimara Ito. My name is Rachel, and I'm calling from the returns team at Nordstrom about the jacket you sent back last week. I wanted to reach you directly, because I have good news and one small question. First, the good news. You told us the zipper was broken, and you were right. The item was defective, so this is not a normal return. That means the fifteen percent restocking fee does not apply to you. I have already removed it from your account. Now, about the refund. Because the jacket was defective, you have a choice that most customers do not get. You can have the full amount back on your original card, or you can take it as store credit, and if you choose store credit, we will add an extra ten percent as an apology. That is entirely up to you. Just so you know, our normal return policy is thirty days with the receipt, but for a defective item there is no time limit at all. One more thing. I saw that you are part of our loyalty program, so I have added the points for this purchase back to your account as well. If you would like the refund on your card, you do not need to do anything. If you would prefer the store credit, just reply to this message or call us back. Thank you, and I'm sorry for the trouble.

Welcome back to The Everyday Traveler. Today, one thing that surprises almost every visitor to the United States: how easy it is to return something. Here is what you need to understand. In a lot of the world, once you buy it, it is yours. In America, the return is part of the deal. Big stores compete on it. So the first rule is simple: keep your receipt, and check the return policy before you buy, not after. Most stores give you thirty days. Some give you a full year. Now, I used to think you had to accept whatever price was on the tag. You do not. Two words changed how I shop here: price match. If you find the same item cheaper somewhere else, show them, and many stores will match it on the spot. And here is the one that people from countries with strong consumer laws get wrong. If something is defective, you are not asking for a favor. You have a right. So do not apologize. Be polite, be calm, but be clear: the item is broken, and you would like a refund or an exchange. Keep the receipt, know the policy, and remember that in an American store, the customer really does have the upper hand. Use it kindly, and use it well.
